title: U.S. Energy Information Administration - EIA - Independent Statistics and Analysis
gist: Educational 'Energy Explained' primer from the EIA defining crude oil and petroleum products — what they are, how they form, and how a refinery turns a barrel of crude into usable products. No market analysis, forecasts, or current data; the one quantitative detail is refinery processing gain (a 42-gallon barrel yields ~45 gallons of products). Essentially a static reference/landing-explainer page rather than a research note.

insights.claim: A U.S. 42-gallon barrel of crude oil yields about 45 gallons of petroleum products in U.S. refineries due to refinery processing gain, and the product mix shifts month-to-month and year-to-year as refineries adjust to demand and profitability.
insights.speaker: EIA — Energy Information Administration
insights.so_what: The ~7% volumetric gain and demand-driven product slate are baseline facts for modeling refinery throughput and crack-spread economics, though this primer carries no current levels to trade on.
insights.quote: A U.S. 42-gallon barrel of crude oil yields about 45 gallons of petroleum products in U.S. refineries because of refinery processing gain.
insights.locator: What petroleum products are made from crude oil?
